title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
In MGTA495 Marketing Analytics, we are asked to build our own website. That makes the setting of this homework unusually practical: suppose my site includes a landing page where visitors can subscribe to a newsletter. A small change in wording may look trivial, but in digital products, seemingly minor interface choices often affect behavior in measurable ways.
To make that concrete, I compare two calls to action:

CTA A: Sign up for our newsletter here!
CTA B: Stay tuned by signing up!

This is a standard A/B test. Visitors are randomly assigned to one of the two versions, and I compare the sign-up rates across groups. The role of random assignment is simple but essential: it allows me to attribute differences in outcomes to the CTA itself rather than to systematic differences in who happened to see each version.
Using this setup, I walk through several core ideas from classical frequentist statistics: the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of a two-sample test, and the danger of peeking at results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each outcome can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable. Let the sign-up probability under CTA A be \(\pi_A\) and the sign-up probability under CTA B be \(\pi_B\).
For each visitor:

outcome = 1 if the visitor signs up
outcome = 0 if the visitor does not sign up

The quantity I care about is the difference in sign-up rates:
\(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\)
A positive value means CTA A performs better; a negative value means CTA B performs better.
In the sample, I estimate this quantity with the difference in sample means:
\(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\)
Because the outcomes are binary, each sample mean is simply a sample proportion. That is, \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\), so \(\hat{\theta}\) is the observed difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment, the true sign-up probabilities are unknown. Here, I deliberately choose them in advance so that I can evaluate how the estimator behaves when the underlying truth is known.
I set the sign-up probability under CTA A to 0.22 and under CTA B to 0.18, so the true treatment effect is 0.04. I then simulate 1,000 visitors in each group. Because the data are generated randomly, the realized sample rates will not match the population values exactly, but they should be reasonably close.

n_a = 1000
n_b = 1000

pi_a = 0.22
pi_b = 0.18
theta_true = pi_a - pi_b

cta_a = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_a, size=n_a)
cta_b = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_b, size=n_b)

df_ab = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "group": (["A"] * n_a) + (["B"] * n_b),
        "signup": np.concatenate([cta_a, cta_b]),
    }
)

The simulated sample offers a first look at the experiment. Due to random variation, the observed rates are not exactly 0.22 and 0.18, which is precisely why inference matters in the first place.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers explains why large samples are so valuable in experimentation. As sample size grows, sample averages stabilize around their population counterparts. In this context, that means the estimated difference in sign-up rates should move closer to the true treatment effect as more observations accumulate.
The figure below plots the running average of the element-wise differences between the two groups. At the beginning, the path is noisy because each additional observation has a large influence. As the sample grows, that influence fades and the estimate becomes more stable. The main takeaway is not that noise disappears, but that its impact becomes progressively less dominant.

diff_vec = cta_a - cta_b
cum_mean = np.cumsum(diff_vec) / np.arange(1, len(diff_vec) + 1)









Figure 1: Running average of the difference in outcomes across CTA A and CTA B.





The figure behaves exactly as the Law of Large Numbers suggests. Early on, the running average moves sharply because the sample is tiny. Later, the curve settles and drifts toward the true effect. In practical terms, this is why experimentation improves with scale: larger samples reduce the influence of noise on the estimate.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate tells me what I observed. A standard error tells me how much that estimate would be expected to vary across repeated samples. That distinction matters: decision-making should depend not only on the estimated effect, but also on how precisely it has been measured.
The bootstrap provides a practical way to estimate this uncertainty. Instead of relying only on a closed-form formula, I repeatedly resample the observed data with replacement, recompute the treatment effect, and use the variability across those resampled estimates as an estimate of the standard error.
In this example, the bootstrapped and analytical standard errors are very close. That is reassuring. It suggests that the simulation, the resampling logic, and the textbook variance formula are all telling a consistent story. Using the bootstrapped standard error, I then construct a 95% confidence interval for the treatment effect. The interval summarizes the range of plausible values for the true difference in sign-up rates given the observed data.

n_boot = 1000
theta_boot = np.empty(n_boot)

for i in range(n_boot):
    a_star = rng.choice(cta_a, size=n_a, replace=True)
    b_star = rng.choice(cta_b, size=n_b, replace=True)
    theta_boot[i] = a_star.mean() - b_star.mean()

theta_hat = cta_a.mean() - cta_b.mean()
se_boot = theta_boot.std(ddof=1)

pi_hat_a = cta_a.mean()
pi_hat_b = cta_b.mean()

se_analytic = np.sqrt(
    (pi_hat_a * (1 - pi_hat_a) / n_a)
    + (pi_hat_b * (1 - pi_hat_b) / n_b)
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

Figure 2: Bootstrap distribution of the estimated treatment effect.





Using the bootstrap standard error, the 95% confidence interval takes the form
\([\hat{\theta} - 1.96 \cdot SE_{boot}, \hat{\theta} + 1.96 \cdot SE_{boot}]\)
In plain language, this interval gives a reasonable range of plausible values for the true treatment effect. It shifts the discussion from a single point estimate to a more honest summary of both effect and uncertainty.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem is one of the main reasons large-sample inference works. Even though each individual outcome here is Bernoulli rather than Normally distributed, the sampling distribution of the sample mean—and therefore the difference in sample means—becomes approximately Normal as the sample size increases.
The four histograms below show this directly. For small samples, the distribution of the estimator is rougher and less stable. As sample size increases, it becomes smoother, more symmetric, and more concentrated around the true effect. This is exactly the behavior that makes Normal-based inference reasonable in large samples.

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_rep = 1000

clt_frames = []

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_sim = np.empty(n_rep)

    for i in range(n_rep):
        a_sim = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_a, size=n)
        b_sim = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_b, size=n)
        theta_sim[i] = a_sim.mean() - b_sim.mean()

    clt_frames.append(
        pd.DataFrame(
            {
                "n": n,
                "theta_hat": theta_sim,
            }
        )
    )

df_clt = pd.concat(clt_frames, ignore_index=True)









Figure 3: Sampling distributions of the difference in means under different sample sizes.





The visual pattern is clear: more data produce a smoother and more concentrated sampling distribution. That is not merely a theoretical curiosity. It is the reason later sections can rely on Normal approximations when making inferential statements.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Once the sampling distribution of the estimator is approximately Normal, I can move from estimation to formal testing. The null hypothesis is that the two CTAs perform equally well, so the treatment effect is zero. The alternative is that the effect is nonzero.
To evaluate this, I standardize the observed estimate by dividing it by its standard error. This creates a test statistic that is approximately standard Normal under the null. That approximation is what allows me to calculate a p-value.
Although this is often loosely called a “t-test,” that label is not exact in this setting. The classical t-test relies on Normal data and an exact finite-sample t-distribution. Here the data are Bernoulli, and the reference distribution comes from the Central Limit Theorem, so the logic is closer to a large-sample z-test. In practice the distinction is minor for large samples, but conceptually it is worth being precise about why the procedure works.

z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytic
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

The result should be interpreted in the usual frequentist way. A large absolute test statistic means the observed estimate sits far from zero relative to its own sampling variability. A small p-value indicates that such a result would be unlikely under the null hypothesis of equal CTA performance.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
A useful insight is that the same two-group comparison can be written as a regression. Once the outcomes from both CTA groups are stacked into one dataset, I can regress the signup indicator on a treatment dummy for whether the visitor saw CTA A.
In that regression, the intercept is the mean signup rate for group B, while the coefficient on the treatment dummy is the difference between groups A and B. In other words, the treatment coefficient is numerically the same estimand as the difference in sample proportions from the two-sample test.
This equivalence matters because regression is not a different method here—it is a more general framework containing the same method. In the simple two-group case, both approaches deliver essentially the same result. In more realistic settings, however, the regression form becomes especially useful because it extends naturally to controls, interactions, and more complex experimental designs.

df_reg = (
    df_ab
    .assign(d=(df_ab["group"] == "A").astype(int))
)

y_var = df_reg["signup"]
x_vars = sm.add_constant(df_reg[["d"]])

ols_mod = sm.OLS(y_var, x_vars).fit()

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Suppose the experimenter does not wait until all observations are collected, but instead checks significance repeatedly as data arrive. That practice is tempting because it feels efficient. In reality, it changes the statistical meaning of the test.
A single hypothesis test at the 5% significance level has a 5% Type I error rate under the null. But repeated peeking creates multiple chances to obtain a significant result by luck alone. The overall false positive rate is therefore no longer 5%; it becomes materially larger.
The simulation below makes this inflation visible. Under a true null where both CTAs have the same sign-up probability, repeatedly checking for significance after every 100 observations per group leads to a much higher probability of incorrectly declaring a winner. The implication is straightforward: disciplined experimentation requires disciplined stopping rules. Without them, apparent “wins” can be statistical artifacts rather than real effects.

pi_null = 0.20
n_total = 1000
peek_points = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100)
n_exp = 10000

peek_sig = np.zeros(n_exp, dtype=int)

for exp in range(n_exp):
    a_null = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_null, size=n_total)
    b_null = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_null, size=n_total)

    found_sig = 0

    for n in peek_points:
        a_now = a_null[:n]
        b_now = b_null[:n]

        theta_now = a_now.mean() - b_now.mean()

        se_now = np.sqrt(
            (a_now.mean() * (1 - a_now.mean()) / n)
            + (b_now.mean() * (1 - b_now.mean()) / n)
        )

        if se_now > 0:
            z_now = theta_now / se_now
            p_now = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_now)))

            if p_now < 0.05:
                found_sig = 1
                break

    peek_sig[exp] = found_sig

false_positive_rate_peeking = peek_sig.mean()










metric
value




0
Nominal false positive rate (single test)
0.050


1
Empirical false positive rate with peeking
0.194















Figure 4: False positive rate under the null: single test versus repeated peeking.





This is the most practical lesson in the post. It is easy to believe that “checking one more time” is harmless. The simulation shows otherwise: repeated testing without adjustment meaningfully inflates the chance of a false discovery.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
This A/B testing exercise brings several foundational ideas together in one applied setting. The Law of Large Numbers explains why estimates stabilize. The bootstrap provides a practical measure of uncertainty. The Central Limit Theorem justifies large-sample Normal inference. Hypothesis testing formalizes the decision rule. Regression reveals that the two-sample comparison is part of a broader modeling framework. And the peeking simulation shows that sound inference depends not only on formulas, but also on experimental discipline.
That last point is especially relevant in product and marketing analytics. Running an experiment is not difficult. Running one in a way that preserves the meaning of the inference is the real standard.

Suppose the experimenter does not wait until all observations are collected, but instead checks significance repeatedly as data arrive. That practice is tempting because it feels efficient. In reality, it changes the statistical meaning of the test.
A single hypothesis test at the 5% significance level has a 5% Type I error rate under the null. But repeated peeking creates multiple chances to obtain a significant result by luck alone. The overall false positive rate is therefore no longer 5%; it becomes materially larger.
The simulation below makes this inflation visible. Under a true null where both CTAs have the same sign-up probability, repeatedly checking for significance after every 100 observations per group leads to a much higher probability of incorrectly declaring a winner. The implication is straightforward: disciplined experimentation requires disciplined stopping rules. Without them, apparent “wins” can be statistical artifacts rather than real effects.

pi_null = 0.20
n_total = 1000
peek_points = np.arange(100, n_total + 1, 100)
n_exp = 10000

peek_sig = np.zeros(n_exp, dtype=int)

for exp in range(n_exp):
    a_null = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_null, size=n_total)
    b_null = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_null, size=n_total)

    found_sig = 0

    for n in peek_points:
        a_now = a_null[:n]
        b_now = b_null[:n]

        theta_now = a_now.mean() - b_now.mean()

        se_now = np.sqrt(
            (a_now.mean() * (1 - a_now.mean()) / n)
            + (b_now.mean() * (1 - b_now.mean()) / n)
        )

        if se_now > 0:
            z_now = theta_now / se_now
            p_now = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_now)))

            if p_now < 0.05:
                found_sig = 1
                break

    peek_sig[exp] = found_sig

false_positive_rate_peeking = peek_sig.mean()

value

Nominal false positive rate (single test)
0.050


1
Empirical false positive rate with peeking
0.194















Figure 4: False positive rate under the null: single test versus repeated peeking.





This is the most practical lesson in the post. It is easy to believe that “checking one more time” is harmless. The simulation shows otherwise: repeated testing without adjustment meaningfully inflates the chance of a false discovery.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Conclusion
This A/B testing exercise brings several foundational ideas together in one applied setting. The Law of Large Numbers explains why estimates stabilize. The bootstrap provides a practical measure of uncertainty. The Central Limit Theorem justifies large-sample Normal inference. Hypothesis testing formalizes the decision rule. Regression reveals that the two-sample comparison is part of a broader modeling framework. And the peeking simulation shows that sound inference depends not only on formulas, but also on experimental discipline.
That last point is especially relevant in product and marketing analytics. Running an experiment is not difficult. Running one in a way that preserves the meaning of the inference is the real standard.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
In MGTA495 Marketing Analytics, we are asked to build our own website. That makes the setting of this homework unusually practical: suppose my site includes a landing page where visitors can subscribe to a newsletter. A small change in wording may look trivial, but in digital products, seemingly minor interface choices often affect behavior in measurable ways.
To make that concrete, I compare two calls to action:

CTA A: Sign up for our newsletter here!
CTA B: Stay tuned by signing up!

This is a standard A/B test. Visitors are randomly assigned to one of the two versions, and I compare the sign-up rates across groups. The role of random assignment is simple but essential: it allows me to attribute differences in outcomes to the CTA itself rather than to systematic differences in who happened to see each version.
Using this setup, I walk through several core ideas from classical frequentist statistics: the Law of Large Numbers, bootstrap standard errors, the Central Limit Theorem, hypothesis testing, the regression interpretation of a two-sample test, and the danger of peeking at results too early.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each visitor either signs up or does not sign up, so each outcome can be modeled as a Bernoulli random variable. Let the sign-up probability under CTA A be \(\pi_A\) and the sign-up probability under CTA B be \(\pi_B\).
For each visitor:

outcome = 1 if the visitor signs up
outcome = 0 if the visitor does not sign up

The quantity I care about is the difference in sign-up rates:
\(\theta = \pi_A - \pi_B\)
A positive value means CTA A performs better; a negative value means CTA B performs better.
In the sample, I estimate this quantity with the difference in sample means:
\(\hat{\theta} = \bar{X}_A - \bar{X}_B\)
Because the outcomes are binary, each sample mean is simply a sample proportion. That is, \(\bar{X}_A = \hat{\pi}_A\) and \(\bar{X}_B = \hat{\pi}_B\), so \(\hat{\theta}\) is the observed difference in sign-up rates between the two CTAs.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In a real experiment, the true sign-up probabilities are unknown. Here, I deliberately choose them in advance so that I can evaluate how the estimator behaves when the underlying truth is known.
I set the sign-up probability under CTA A to 0.22 and under CTA B to 0.18, so the true treatment effect is 0.04. I then simulate 1,000 visitors in each group. Because the data are generated randomly, the realized sample rates will not match the population values exactly, but they should be reasonably close.

n_a = 1000
n_b = 1000

pi_a = 0.22
pi_b = 0.18
theta_true = pi_a - pi_b

cta_a = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_a, size=n_a)
cta_b = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_b, size=n_b)

df_ab = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "group": (["A"] * n_a) + (["B"] * n_b),
        "signup": np.concatenate([cta_a, cta_b]),
    }
)

The simulated sample offers a first look at the experiment. Due to random variation, the observed rates are not exactly 0.22 and 0.18, which is precisely why inference matters in the first place.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers explains why large samples are so valuable in experimentation. As sample size grows, sample averages stabilize around their population counterparts. In this context, that means the estimated difference in sign-up rates should move closer to the true treatment effect as more observations accumulate.
The figure below plots the running average of the element-wise differences between the two groups. At the beginning, the path is noisy because each additional observation has a large influence. As the sample grows, that influence fades and the estimate becomes more stable. The main takeaway is not that noise disappears, but that its impact becomes progressively less dominant.

diff_vec = cta_a - cta_b
cum_mean = np.cumsum(diff_vec) / np.arange(1, len(diff_vec) + 1)









Figure 1: Running average of the difference in outcomes across CTA A and CTA B.





The figure behaves exactly as the Law of Large Numbers suggests. Early on, the running average moves sharply because the sample is tiny. Later, the curve settles and drifts toward the true effect. In practical terms, this is why experimentation improves with scale: larger samples reduce the influence of noise on the estimate.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
A point estimate tells me what I observed. A standard error tells me how much that estimate would be expected to vary across repeated samples. That distinction matters: decision-making should depend not only on the estimated effect, but also on how precisely it has been measured.
The bootstrap provides a practical way to estimate this uncertainty. Instead of relying only on a closed-form formula, I repeatedly resample the observed data with replacement, recompute the treatment effect, and use the variability across those resampled estimates as an estimate of the standard error.
In this example, the bootstrapped and analytical standard errors are very close. That is reassuring. It suggests that the simulation, the resampling logic, and the textbook variance formula are all telling a consistent story. Using the bootstrapped standard error, I then construct a 95% confidence interval for the treatment effect. The interval summarizes the range of plausible values for the true difference in sign-up rates given the observed data.

n_boot = 1000
theta_boot = np.empty(n_boot)

for i in range(n_boot):
    a_star = rng.choice(cta_a, size=n_a, replace=True)
    b_star = rng.choice(cta_b, size=n_b, replace=True)
    theta_boot[i] = a_star.mean() - b_star.mean()

theta_hat = cta_a.mean() - cta_b.mean()
se_boot = theta_boot.std(ddof=1)

pi_hat_a = cta_a.mean()
pi_hat_b = cta_b.mean()

se_analytic = np.sqrt(
    (pi_hat_a * (1 - pi_hat_a) / n_a)
    + (pi_hat_b * (1 - pi_hat_b) / n_b)
)

ci_lower = theta_hat - 1.96 * se_boot
ci_upper = theta_hat + 1.96 * se_boot

Figure 2: Bootstrap distribution of the estimated treatment effect.





Using the bootstrap standard error, the 95% confidence interval takes the form
\([\hat{\theta} - 1.96 \cdot SE_{boot}, \hat{\theta} + 1.96 \cdot SE_{boot}]\)
In plain language, this interval gives a reasonable range of plausible values for the true treatment effect. It shifts the discussion from a single point estimate to a more honest summary of both effect and uncertainty.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem is one of the main reasons large-sample inference works. Even though each individual outcome here is Bernoulli rather than Normally distributed, the sampling distribution of the sample mean—and therefore the difference in sample means—becomes approximately Normal as the sample size increases.
The four histograms below show this directly. For small samples, the distribution of the estimator is rougher and less stable. As sample size increases, it becomes smoother, more symmetric, and more concentrated around the true effect. This is exactly the behavior that makes Normal-based inference reasonable in large samples.

sample_sizes = [25, 50, 100, 500]
n_rep = 1000

clt_frames = []

for n in sample_sizes:
    theta_sim = np.empty(n_rep)

    for i in range(n_rep):
        a_sim = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_a, size=n)
        b_sim = rng.binomial(n=1, p=pi_b, size=n)
        theta_sim[i] = a_sim.mean() - b_sim.mean()

    clt_frames.append(
        pd.DataFrame(
            {
                "n": n,
                "theta_hat": theta_sim,
            }
        )
    )

df_clt = pd.concat(clt_frames, ignore_index=True)









Figure 3: Sampling distributions of the difference in means under different sample sizes.





The visual pattern is clear: more data produce a smoother and more concentrated sampling distribution. That is not merely a theoretical curiosity. It is the reason later sections can rely on Normal approximations when making inferential statements.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
Once the sampling distribution of the estimator is approximately Normal, I can move from estimation to formal testing. The null hypothesis is that the two CTAs perform equally well, so the treatment effect is zero. The alternative is that the effect is nonzero.
To evaluate this, I standardize the observed estimate by dividing it by its standard error. This creates a test statistic that is approximately standard Normal under the null. That approximation is what allows me to calculate a p-value.
Although this is often loosely called a “t-test,” that label is not exact in this setting. The classical t-test relies on Normal data and an exact finite-sample t-distribution. Here the data are Bernoulli, and the reference distribution comes from the Central Limit Theorem, so the logic is closer to a large-sample z-test. In practice the distinction is minor for large samples, but conceptually it is worth being precise about why the procedure works.

z_stat = theta_hat / se_analytic
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z_stat)))

The result should be interpreted in the usual frequentist way. A large absolute test statistic means the observed estimate sits far from zero relative to its own sampling variability. A small p-value indicates that such a result would be unlikely under the null hypothesis of equal CTA performance.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
A useful insight is that the same two-group comparison can be written as a regression. Once the outcomes from both CTA groups are stacked into one dataset, I can regress the signup indicator on a treatment dummy for whether the visitor saw CTA A.
In that regression, the intercept is the mean signup rate for group B, while the coefficient on the treatment dummy is the difference between groups A and B. In other words, the treatment coefficient is numerically the same estimand as the difference in sample proportions from the two-sample test.
This equivalence matters because regression is not a different method here—it is a more general framework containing the same method. In the simple two-group case, both approaches deliver essentially the same result. In more realistic settings, however, the regression form becomes especially useful because it extends naturally to controls, interactions, and more complex experimental designs.

df_reg = (
    df_ab
    .assign(d=(df_ab["group"] == "A").astype(int))
)

y_var = df_reg["signup"]
x_vars = sm.add_constant(df_reg[["d"]])

ols_mod = sm.OLS(y_var, x_vars).fit()

Any small gap in standard errors comes from the fact that the regression framework and the two-sample formula are not written in exactly the same way, even though they tell essentially the same inferential story here.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
In Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania, Card and Krueger (1994) study whether New Jersey’s April 1992 minimum-wage increase from $4.25 to $5.05 reduced employment in fast-food restaurants. Their core design compares stores in New Jersey to similar stores in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change over the same period.
This paper is a classic empirical challenge to the standard competitive prediction that a binding minimum wage must reduce employment. Its identification strategy is a simple but powerful difference-in-differences design: compare employment before and after the policy in New Jersey, then subtract the same before-after change in Pennsylvania.
In this post, I replicate a key employment result from the paper, reproduce part of the main regression table, and add one extra comparison showing why the difference-in-differences logic matters more than a naive cross-sectional comparison.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
In Minimum Wages and Employment: A Case Study of the Fast-Food Industry in New Jersey and Pennsylvania, Card and Krueger (1994) study whether New Jersey’s April 1992 minimum-wage increase from $4.25 to $5.05 reduced employment in fast-food restaurants. Their core design compares stores in New Jersey to similar stores in eastern Pennsylvania, where the minimum wage did not change over the same period.
This paper is a classic empirical challenge to the standard competitive prediction that a binding minimum wage must reduce employment. Its identification strategy is a simple but powerful difference-in-differences design: compare employment before and after the policy in New Jersey, then subtract the same before-after change in Pennsylvania.
In this post, I replicate a key employment result from the paper, reproduce part of the main regression table, and add one extra comparison showing why the difference-in-differences logic matters more than a naive cross-sectional comparison.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
What the Authors Are Studying
The causal question is straightforward:

Did New Jersey’s 1992 minimum-wage increase reduce fast-food employment relative to a comparable control group?

The treatment group is fast-food restaurants in New Jersey. The control group is fast-food restaurants in eastern Pennsylvania. The main outcome is full-time-equivalent employment (FTE) per store.
The key identifying assumption is parallel trends: absent the New Jersey wage increase, employment in NJ and PA would have changed at the same rate over the same period. If NJ stores were already trending differently for unrelated reasons — say, a regional economic shock — the DiD estimate would conflate the policy effect with that pre-existing divergence. The paper cannot test this directly (the counterfactual is unobserved), but the plausibility of the assumption is what makes eastern Pennsylvania a credible control: it is geographically adjacent and shares the same fast-food chains and labor market conditions.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
Data and Setup
The public replication files contain a flat-file dataset with 410 observations, along with a codebook and a SAS checking script. The codebook shows the original variables used in the article, including first- and second-wave employment counts, starting wages, ownership status, region indicators, store chain, and several price variables.

The paper’s main result is in Table 3. The key comparison is the change in FTE employment in New Jersey relative to the change in Pennsylvania.

Interpretation
My replicated difference-in-differences estimate is about 2.75 FTE employees, very close to the paper’s reported relative gain of 2.76. That means employment in New Jersey increased relative to Pennsylvania after the minimum-wage hike, rather than falling.
This is the central empirical surprise of the paper. A textbook competitive model would predict a negative effect. Card and Krueger instead find no evidence of employment loss in this setting.
My replicated estimates closely match the published point estimates, so the core empirical conclusion is successfully reproduced.

So the regression-adjusted version tells the same basic story as the raw difference-in-differences: I find no evidence that the employment effect is negative.
My replicated estimates closely match the published point estimates, so the core empirical conclusion is successfully reproduced.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
section: Additional Analysis: Naive Comparison vs. Difference-in-Differences
text:
Additional Analysis: Naive Comparison vs. Difference-in-Differences
For the extra piece, I compare a naive post-period cross-state comparison with the actual difference-in-differences estimate.



Estimate
Value




Pre-period NJ - PA difference
-2.89


Post-period NJ - PA difference
-0.14


Difference-in-differences
2.75




Why this matters
A naive post-treatment comparison is not causal because New Jersey and Pennsylvania were already different before treatment. In the raw data, New Jersey stores started smaller on average. Difference-in-differences corrects for that baseline gap by comparing changes, not just levels.
This comparison shows why a post-period cross-sectional gap is not a causal estimate: New Jersey stores already differed from Pennsylvania stores before the policy.
That is exactly why this paper became such a famous applied example. Its result is not driven by a simple treated-versus-control comparison, but by a quasi-experimental before-after design with a nearby control group.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
This replication reproduces the main empirical result of Card and Krueger (1994): I find no evidence that New Jersey’s 1992 minimum-wage increase reduced fast-food employment relative to Pennsylvania. My replicated difference-in-differences estimate is very close to the published result, and the regression specifications tell the same story.
The most important lesson is methodological as much as substantive. If I only looked at post-treatment differences, I would miss the causal logic. The value of the paper comes from the design: compare New Jersey to a plausible control group, compare before to after, and focus on the relative change.
That is also why this paper fits so well into the broader causal inference framework from class. It is a clean, intuitive example of how a simple quasi-experimental design can overturn a strong theoretical prior.

title: Replicating Card & Krueger (1994)

text:

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

from scipy.special import gammaln

sns.set_theme(style="ticks")
BLUE = "#6b9dc2"
RUST = "#e07b54"

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Introduction
Blueprinty makes software that helps engineering firms prepare patent applications for the US Patent and Trademark Office. Its marketing team wants to claim that customers win more patents — a compelling pitch, but a non-trivial one to substantiate.
The cleanest test would be a before-and-after study for each firm, but no such data exists. Instead, Blueprinty has collected a cross-section of 1,500 mature engineering firms, recording patent awards over the last five years alongside each firm’s region, age, and customer status. The complication is that customers are self-selected: firms that buy patent-application software likely differ from those that do not — in region, maturity, and sophistication — and those same differences independently predict patent success. A raw count comparison therefore conflates the software’s effect with pre-existing firm characteristics. The analysis below controls for age and region to isolate the association that remains.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Exploring the Data


Show code
df = pd.read_csv("data/blueprinty.csv")
df['customer_label'] = df['iscustomer'].map({0: 'Non-Customer', 1: 'Customer'})

mean_patents = df.groupby('customer_label')['patents'].mean()
mean_age     = df.groupby('customer_label')['age'].mean()



Patent Counts by Customer Status


Show code
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 4))
x_min, x_max = df['patents'].min() - 0.5, df['patents'].max() + 0.5

for ax, (label, color) in zip(axes, [('Non-Customer', BLUE), ('Customer', RUST)]):

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
section: A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood
text:
A Simple Poisson Model via Maximum Likelihood

Why Poisson?
Patent counts are non-negative integers. The Normal distribution is a poor fit: it places positive probability on negative and fractional values and is symmetric, whereas count data tend to be right-skewed. The Poisson distribution is the natural starting point, assigning probability
\[f(Y \mid \lambda) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{Y}}{Y!}, \qquad Y = 0,\, 1,\, 2,\, \ldots\]
where \(\lambda > 0\) governs both the mean and the variance of the distribution.


Log-Likelihood Derivation
For \(n\) iid observations \(Y_1,\ldots,Y_n \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\), the joint likelihood is:
\[L_n(\lambda) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^{Y_i}}{Y_i!}
= e^{-n\lambda}\,\lambda^{\sum_i Y_i}\prod_{i=1}^n \frac{1}{Y_i!}\]
Taking logarithms yields the log-likelihood:
\[\ell_n(\lambda) = -n\lambda + \Bigl(\sum_{i=1}^n Y_i\Bigr)\log\lambda - \sum_{i=1}^n \log(Y_i!)\]


Coding the Log-Likelihood


Show code
def poisson_loglikelihood(lam, Y):
    """Log-likelihood for an iid sample Y ~ Poisson(lambda)."""
    return np.sum(-lam + Y * np.log(lam) - gammaln(Y + 1))




Plotting the Log-Likelihood


Show code
Y       = df['patents'].values
lambdas = np.linspace(0.5, 8, 500)
ll_vals = [poisson_loglikelihood(l, Y) for l in lambdas]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
ax.plot(lambdas, ll_vals, color=BLUE, linewidth=2.2)
ax.axvline(Y.mean(), color=RUST, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8,
           label=fr'$\bar{{Y}}$ = {Y.mean():.3f}')
ax.set_xlabel(r'$\lambda$', fontsize=13)
ax.set_ylabel(r'$\ell_n(\lambda)$', fontsize=13)
ax.set_title(r'Poisson Log-Likelihood as a Function of $\lambda$', fontsize=13)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(False)
sns.despine(ax=ax)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









The curve is concave with a single peak. The MLE can be read directly off the plot: find the \(\lambda\) on the horizontal axis below the maximum, which coincides with the sample mean (dashed line).


Analytical MLE
Differentiating and setting equal to zero:
\[\frac{d\ell_n}{d\lambda} = -n + \frac{1}{\lambda}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i = 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hat{\lambda}_{\text{MLE}} = \bar{Y}\]
Since \(\mathbb{E}[Y] = \lambda\) under the Poisson model, estimating \(\lambda\) by the sample mean is both the MLE and the intuitively natural choice.


Numerical Confirmation


Show code
result_simple = minimize_scalar(
    fun    = lambda l: -poisson_loglikelihood(l, Y),
    bounds = (0.01, 20),
    method = 'bounded'
)

print(f"Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = {result_simple.x:.6f}")
print(f"Sample mean:    Ȳ  = {Y.mean():.6f}")


Numerical MLE:  λ̂ = 3.684667
Sample mean:    Ȳ  = 3.684667


The optimizer returns a value numerically identical to \(\bar{Y}\), confirming the closed-form derivation. Any residual discrepancy is a product of the optimizer’s step-size tolerance, not any mathematical difference.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
Poisson Regression

Model Setup
A single shared \(\lambda\) is too coarse: a 40-year-old Northeast firm and a 15-year-old Midwest firm should have different expected patent rates. Poisson regression lets the rate vary across firms as a function of their characteristics:
\[Y_i \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda_i), \qquad \lambda_i = \exp(X_i'\beta)\]
The exponential link is critical: \(X_i'\beta\) is unbounded, but \(\lambda_i\) must be strictly positive. Exponentiation maps \(\mathbb{R} \to (0,\infty)\), enforcing positivity without constraints. The log-likelihood becomes:
\[\ell_n(\beta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\Bigl[-\exp(X_i'\beta) + Y_i(X_i'\beta) - \log(Y_i!)\Bigr]\]


Log-Likelihood Function


Show code
def poisson_regression_loglikelihood(beta, Y, X):
    """Log-likelihood for Poisson regression: Y_i ~ Poisson(exp(X_i @ beta))."""
    xb = X @ beta
    return np.sum(-np.exp(xb) + Y * xb - gammaln(Y + 1))




Design Matrix


Show code
# Northeast is the reference region
regions_to_include = ['Midwest', 'Northwest', 'South', 'Southwest']

X_df = pd.DataFrame({'intercept': 1.0,
                      'age':       df['age'],
                      'age_sq':    df['age'] ** 2})
for r in regions_to_include:
    X_df[r] = (df['region'] == r).astype(float)
X_df['iscustomer'] = df['iscustomer'].astype(float)

X         = X_df.values
col_names = X_df.columns.tolist()
print("Columns:", col_names)
print("Shape:  ", X.shape)


Columns: ['intercept', 'age', 'age_sq', 'Midwest', 'Northwest', 'South', 'Southwest', 'iscustomer']
Shape:   (1500, 8)


Age squared is included alongside age because the patent–age relationship is likely hump-shaped: firms may ramp up patenting as they mature and accumulate expertise, then slow as their core innovations are already protected. A quadratic term captures this non-linearity without imposing a rigid functional form. One region is dropped (Northeast) because including all five dummies alongside an intercept would introduce perfect collinearity — the five indicators sum to 1 for every observation, exactly replicating the intercept column. All region coefficients are interpreted relative to this Northeast baseline.


Maximum Likelihood Estimation


Show code
beta0    = np.zeros(len(col_names))
beta0[0] = np.log(Y.mean())          # warm-start intercept

result   = minimize(lambda b: -poisson_regression_loglikelihood(b, Y, X),
                    beta0, method='BFGS')

beta_hat = result.x
se_hat   = np.sqrt(np.diag(result.hess_inv))

results_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Coefficient': beta_hat,
    'Std Error':   se_hat,
    'z-value':     beta_hat / se_hat,
}, index=col_names).round(4)

display(results_df.style
        .format({'Coefficient': '{:.4f}', 'Std Error': '{:.4f}', 'z-value': '{:.3f}'})
        .set_caption("Table 1. MLE Estimates — Poisson Regression (BFGS)"))

The statsmodels GLM coefficients match our hand-coded estimates to four decimal places. Standard errors are close but not identical: statsmodels derives them from the exact observed Fisher information evaluated at the MLE, while BFGS accumulates an approximate inverse Hessian over the optimization path. The GLM standard errors are generally preferred; the BFGS approximation is a convenient shortcut that performs well in practice.


Interpreting the Coefficients
In a Poisson regression, coefficients enter multiplicatively: a coefficient \(\hat\beta_k\) implies that a one-unit increase in \(X_k\) multiplies \(\lambda_i\) by \(\exp(\hat\beta_k)\), all else held constant.

iscustomer (0.2076): The key coefficient. Being a Blueprinty customer is associated with \(\exp(0\.2076) \approx\) 1.231 — a 23% increase in expected patents after controlling for age and region. The z-value of 6.60 indicates this estimate is statistically well-separated from zero.
age and age_sq: A positive linear and negative quadratic coefficient traces an inverted-U relationship — patent activity rises with firm maturity then tapers for the oldest firms, consistent with diminishing innovation returns late in a corporate lifecycle.
Regional dummies: Midwest, Northwest, South, and Southwest all carry small negative-to-zero coefficients relative to the Northeast, confirming the Northeast as the most patent-active region in the sample even after conditioning on age and customer status.

title: Poisson Regression and Maximum Likelihood Estimation
text:
The Effect of Blueprinty’s Software

Counterfactual Simulation
Because the model is multiplicative, the iscustomer coefficient cannot be read directly as additional patents. A more interpretable estimate comes from a counterfactual simulation: predict each firm’s expected patent count twice — once with every firm’s customer status set to 0, and once with it set to 1 — while holding all other characteristics (age, region) at their observed values. The average difference is the estimated effect of Blueprinty’s software.


Show code
cust_col = col_names.index('iscustomer')

X0 = X.copy(); X0[:, cust_col] = 0.0
X1 = X.copy(); X1[:, cust_col] = 1.0

y0_hat     = np.exp(X0 @ beta_hat)
y1_hat     = np.exp(X1 @ beta_hat)
avg_effect = (y1_hat - y0_hat).mean()

print(f"Avg predicted patents — non-customer:   {y0_hat.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Avg predicted patents — customer:       {y1_hat.mean():.4f}")
print(f"Estimated average effect of Blueprinty: +{avg_effect:.4f} patents")


Avg predicted patents — non-customer:   3.4362
Avg predicted patents — customer:       4.2291
Estimated average effect of Blueprinty: +0.7929 patents




Show code
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.histplot(y1_hat - y0_hat, bins=40, color=BLUE,
             edgecolor='white', alpha=0.85, ax=ax)
ax.axvline(avg_effect, color=RUST, linestyle='--', linewidth=1.8,
           label=f'Average effect = {avg_effect:.3f}')
ax.set_xlabel(r"$\hat{y}_1 - \hat{y}_0$ (additional patents per firm)", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Number of firms")
ax.set_title("Firm-Level Counterfactual Effect of Blueprinty Software", fontsize=13)
ax.legend(fontsize=11)
ax.grid(False)
sns.despine(ax=ax)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











Conclusion
Holding firm age and region fixed at each firm’s observed values, adopting Blueprinty is associated with approximately 0.79 additional patents over five years — a 23% gain above the counterfactual baseline of 3.44 patents. The distribution of firm-level differences is tightly clustered because the iscustomer coefficient applies a uniform multiplicative shift; the spread reflects variation in age- and region-driven baselines across firms.
The result supports Blueprinty’s marketing claim, but two caveats matter. First, this is an observational study: firms self-selected into the customer base, and unobserved characteristics — R&D investment, quality of patent counsel, technological complexity — may drive part of the remaining association. A randomised experiment or a credible quasi-experimental design would be required to make a causal claim. Second, Poisson regression assumes equidispersion (mean equals variance), which patent counts often violate; a Negative Binomial model or robust standard errors would be natural robustness checks. Subject to these limitations, the controlled analysis provides meaningful evidence consistent with Blueprinty’s claim that its software helps firms file more successful patent applications.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MaxDiff (also called Best-Worst Scaling) is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. In each screen of the survey, a respondent sees a small subset of items — here, 4 out of 10 — and selects both the most-preferred and least-preferred item from that subset. This process repeats across many screens, so every item appears alongside many different competitors.
The key advantage of MaxDiff over a standard 1–10 rating scale is that it forces genuine trade-offs. Humans are notoriously poor at calibrating numeric scales consistently — one person’s “8” is another’s “6” — but we are quite good at picking a best and a worst when placed side-by-side. MaxDiff eliminates scale-use bias by replacing ratings with comparisons.
In this assignment, the 10 items are the 10 core MSBA courses at UCSD Rady. We estimate students’ relative preference for each class using three methods:

Counts analysis — the percentage of times each class was picked best minus the percentage picked worst, yielding a simple score between −1 and +1.
MNL via maximum likelihood — a multinomial logit model fit by numerical optimization, producing point estimates with classical standard errors.
MNL via Bayesian estimation — the same MNL model fit by a random-walk Metropolis–Hastings sampler, producing full posterior distributions.

All three methods should agree on the broad ranking of classes. They differ in how they quantify preference strength and, critically, in how they communicate uncertainty. Bayesian credible intervals on derived quantities like preference shares are straightforward from MCMC draws, whereas MLE requires the delta method.

text:
The Data


Code
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("data/maxdiff_design_and_choices.csv", encoding="utf-8-sig")
df.columns = df.columns.str.strip()
df = df.rename(columns={"Record ID": "respondent"})

                     usecols=[0, 1], header=0)

# Classify tasks: full best-worst (4 items, items 1-10) vs anchor best-only (2 items, involves Item 11)
task_info = (df.groupby(["respondent", "Task"])
               .agg(n_items=("Item", "count"),
                    has_worst=("Response", lambda x: (x == -1).any()))
               .reset_index())

anchor_task_keys = task_info[task_info["n_items"] == 2][["respondent", "Task"]]

# df_full: only the 680 standard best-worst tasks (items 1-10, J=4)

N       = df["respondent"].nunique()

T_anch  = anchor_task_keys.groupby("respondent").size()

print(f"Anchor best-only tasks/resp: {T_anch.mean():.1f} mean  ({len(anchor_task_keys)} total)")

The dataset contains two task types. Full best-worst tasks (680 total, ~8 per respondent) show 4 of the 10 MSBA courses and collect both a best and a worst pick — this is the standard MaxDiff format analysed below. Anchor best-only tasks (595 total, ~7 per respondent) show one MSBA course alongside a fixed anchor (Item 11) and collect only a best pick; these are an anchored MaxDiff supplement that places the courses on an absolute scale. All MNL analysis below uses only the 680 full tasks.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Counts Analysis
The counts analysis provides a quick, model-free summary of preferences. For each item \(j\), we compute the fraction of times it was picked best and the fraction of times it was picked worst (denominator = number of times that item was shown). The score is:
\[
\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j
\]
A score near +1 means the class is almost always chosen as best whenever shown; near −1 means it is almost always the worst; near 0 means it sits in the undifferentiated middle.


Code
counts = (df_full.groupby("Item")["Response"]
                 .agg(shown="count",
                      best=lambda x: (x == 1).sum(),
                      worst=lambda x: (x == -1).sum())
                 .reset_index())

counts["pct_best"]  = counts["best"]  / counts["shown"]
counts["pct_worst"] = counts["worst"] / counts["shown"]
counts["score"]     = counts["pct_best"] - counts["pct_worst"]
counts = counts.merge(labels, left_on="Item", right_on="item").sort_values("score", ascending=False)

display_cols = ["Item", "label", "shown", "best", "worst", "pct_best", "pct_worst", "score"]
print(counts[display_cols]
      .rename(columns={"label": "Label", "shown": "Shown", "best": "Best",
                       "worst": "Worst", "pct_best": "% Best",
                       "pct_worst": "% Worst", "Score": "score"})

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting a model, we translate the raw survey responses into a form the MNL likelihood can consume. Each MaxDiff task yields two sequential choice observations (per the week-5 MNL slides):

Best pick — from all 4 items shown, which was chosen as best? This is a standard 4-alternative MNL observation.
Worst pick — from the remaining 3 items (best excluded), which was chosen as worst? The key insight is that picking the worst item is equivalent to picking the best item when all utilities are negated. This yields a 3-alternative MNL with flipped signs.

Formally, with utility \(\beta_j\) for item \(j\), the probability that item \(b\) is best from set \(S\) is:
\[
P(\text{best} = b \mid S) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j \in S} \exp(\beta_j)}
\]
Conditional on \(b\) being best, the probability that \(c\) is worst among \(S \setminus \{b\}\) is:
\[
P(\text{worst} = c \mid b, S) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j \in S \setminus \{b\}} \exp(-\beta_j)}
\]
Identifiability: The softmax is invariant to adding a constant to all \(\beta\)’s, so only \(J - 1 = 9\) parameters are identified. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (MGTA 403 as reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
# Pre-build integer arrays for vectorised log-likelihood
best_items_list  = []
best_choice_list = []
worst_items_list = []
worst_choice_list = []

for (_, _task), g in df_full.groupby(["respondent", "Task"]):
    items     = (g["Item"].values - 1)                # 0-indexed, length 4
    best_idx  = int(g.loc[g["Response"] ==  1, "Item"].iat[0]) - 1
    worst_idx = int(g.loc[g["Response"] == -1, "Item"].iat[0]) - 1
    remain    = items[items != best_idx]               # length 3

    best_items_list.append(items)
    best_choice_list.append(best_idx)
    worst_items_list.append(remain)
    worst_choice_list.append(worst_idx)

best_items_arr   = np.array(best_items_list,  dtype=int)  # (n_tasks, 4)
best_choice_arr  = np.array(best_choice_list, dtype=int)  # (n_tasks,)
worst_items_arr  = np.array(worst_items_list, dtype=int)  # (n_tasks, 3)
worst_choice_arr = np.array(worst_choice_list, dtype=int) # (n_tasks,)

print(f"Total tasks: {len(best_choice_arr)}  "
      f"(→ {len(best_choice_arr) * 2} MNL observations)")


Total tasks: 680  (→ 1360 MNL observations)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The log-likelihood summed over all tasks is:
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol\beta) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \Bigl[
  \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\!\!\sum_{j \in S}\!\exp(\beta_j)
  \;-\;
  \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\!\!\!\sum_{j \in S \setminus \{j^*_b\}}\!\exp(-\beta_j)
\Bigr]
\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in each task.


Code
def ll(beta_free: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """Log-likelihood for MaxDiff MNL.

    beta_free: length-9 vector for items 2–10; item 1 is the fixed reference (β₁=0).
    Uses vectorised logsumexp for numerical stability.
    """
    beta = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free])

    # Best pick: log P(best | shown set of 4)
    b_utils  = beta[best_items_arr]                       # (n_tasks, 4)
    ll_best  = beta[best_choice_arr] - logsumexp(b_utils, axis=1)

    # Worst pick: log P(worst | remaining set of 3, negated utilities)
    w_utils  = -beta[worst_items_arr]                     # (n_tasks, 3)
    ll_worst = -beta[worst_choice_arr] - logsumexp(w_utils, axis=1)

    return float(np.sum(ll_best) + np.sum(ll_worst))




Code
result = minimize(
    fun=lambda b: -ll(b),
    x0=np.zeros(9),
    method="BFGS",
    options={"maxiter": 2000},
)

print(f"Converged : {result.success}  (status {result.message})")
print(f"Log-lik at MLE: {-result.fun:.2f}")


Converged : True  (status Optimization terminated successfully.)
Log-lik at MLE: -1572.73




Code
# Numerical Hessian of the negative log-likelihood at the MLE
from statsmodels.tools.numdiff import approx_hess

H   = approx_hess(result.x, lambda b: -ll(b))
cov = np.linalg.inv(H)
se  = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov))

beta_hat = np.concatenate([[0.0], result.x])
se_hat   = np.concatenate([[np.nan], se])

shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum()

mle_df = labels.copy()
mle_df["beta"]  = beta_hat
mle_df["se"]    = se_hat
mle_df["share"] = shares_mle

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation
The Bayesian approach treats \(\boldsymbol\beta\) as a random variable and targets the posterior:
\[
\pi(\boldsymbol\beta \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \propto L(\boldsymbol\beta) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol\beta)
\]
We use a weakly informative prior: \(\boldsymbol\beta \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\) (SD \(\approx 3.16\) per coefficient), which is broadly diffuse relative to the likely signal in the data.


Code
def log_prior(beta_free: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return float(norm.logpdf(beta_free, 0, np.sqrt(10)).sum())

def log_post(beta_free: np.ndarray) -> float:
    return ll(beta_free) + log_prior(beta_free)


def mh_sampler(log_post_fn, beta0, n_iter, step, seed=42):
    """Random-walk Metropolis–Hastings sampler."""
    np.random.seed(seed)
    K      = len(beta0)
    draws  = np.empty((n_iter, K))
    beta   = beta0.copy()
    lp     = log_post_fn(beta)
    accept = 0

    for t in range(n_iter):
        proposal = beta + np.random.normal(0, step, K)
        lp_star  = log_post_fn(proposal)
        if np.log(np.random.uniform()) < lp_star - lp:
            beta   = proposal
            lp     = lp_star
            accept += 1
        draws[t] = beta

    return draws, accept / n_iter




Code
# Quick tuning run to verify acceptance rate
draws_tune, acc_tune = mh_sampler(log_post, result.x, n_iter=2000, step=0.3, seed=0)

Side-by-side comparison of all three methods. Colors identify the same course across panels.




Discussion of the comparison:
All three methods agree strongly at the extremes: Customer Analytics (MGTA 455) and Marketing Analytics (MGTA 495) rank first and second under every method, while the Capstone (MGTA 454) and Analytics Consulting (MGTA 444) consistently occupy the bottom. This stability at the tails is expected — items with high or low utility generate strong, unambiguous choice signals.
Disagreements occur in the middle of the ranking, where courses like Large Data (MGTA 452), Business Analytics (MGTA 453), and Biz Intelligence Systems (MGTA 457) are closely bunched. Small differences in which tasks each item happened to appear in — and alongside which competitors — can flip their orderings. The counts analysis is most susceptible to these accidents.
What MNL adds over counts: The MNL treats every shown-but-not-chosen item as genuine information. When MGTA 452 appears alongside MGTA 455 and is not selected, that updates MGTA 452’s utility estimate downward. Counts analysis ignores this contextual information entirely. MNL is also model-based, making it easier to extend to segment or individual-level models.
What Bayes adds over MLE: The primary gain is direct uncertainty quantification on derived quantities. Preference shares are a nonlinear function of \(\boldsymbol\beta\); their posterior distributions emerge for free from the MCMC draws. With MLE, obtaining intervals on shares requires the delta method, which is cumbersome for nonlinear transformations. Bayesian estimation also provides a natural path to hierarchical (individual-level) models.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion

Substantive findings
MSBA students most strongly prefer the applied marketing courses — Customer Analytics (MGTA 455) and Marketing Analytics (MGTA 495) — unsurprising given that many students enrolled for marketing-analytics roles. AI Math & Programming (MGTA 403) and SQL (MGTA 464) score lowest, likely because they are perceived as obligatory prerequisites rather than career-defining courses. The Capstone (MGTA 454) and Consulting (MGTA 444) also score low, perhaps reflecting anxiety about project-based assessment. One potential surprise: Large Data (MGTA 452) ranks only in the middle despite strong industry demand for big-data skills, suggesting students undervalue it relative to what the job market rewards.


Methodological findings







Method
Best used when…




Counts
You need a quick executive summary — no modeling required, instantly interpretable


MLE MNL
You want rigorous point estimates with classical standard errors; the single best number to report in a paper or slide deck


Bayesian MNL
You need uncertainty on derived quantities (shares, simulations, “what if we add a new item?”), or plan to extend to a hierarchical model estimating individual-level preferences





Caveats
The survey was administered to a self-selected sample of MSBA students — likely a single cohort — so preferences may not generalize across years or programs. Prior coursework, year in the program, and career track all confound responses. Item ordering within screens, while randomized, could still introduce minor anchoring effects.


Future directions
Given more time, the natural next step would be a hierarchical Bayes model estimating individual-level \(\beta_i\) vectors. This would reveal whether the aggregated preferences mask distinct segments (e.g., marketing-track vs. data-engineering-track students) and support personalized course recommendations.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Note



Write a short introduction for the reader that sets the scene. Cover the following points in your own words:

MaxDiff (also called Best-Worst Scaling) is a survey methodology for measuring how strongly people prefer one item over another. On each screen of the survey, the respondent sees a small subset of items (here, 4) and selects the most-preferred and least-preferred item from that subset. They repeat this over many screens.
Why MaxDiff (and not, e.g., a 1–10 rating scale)? Humans are good at trade-offs — picking a best and a worst — but unreliable at calibrating numeric scales. MaxDiff forces real differentiation between items and avoids scale-use bias. (See the week-4 “MaxDiff” deck for more.)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices






Note




Before fitting an MNL, explain to the reader how the MaxDiff survey data converts into MNL choice observations. Reference the conjoint/MNL slides from week 5.
The MNL model assigns each item \(j\) a utility coefficient \(\beta_j\). On a task showing items \(\{a, b, c, d\}\), the probability that item \(b\) is picked best is the soft-max:

\[
P(\text{best} = b) \;=\; \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\exp(\beta_a) + \exp(\beta_b) + \exp(\beta_c) + \exp(\beta_d)}
\]

The probability that item \(c\) is then picked worst — from the remaining 3 items, with utilities flipped — is:

\[
P(\text{worst} = c \mid \text{best} = b) \;=\; \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\exp(-\beta_a) + \exp(-\beta_c) + \exp(-\beta_d)}
\]
So each task gives two MNL observations: the best-pick from the full set, and the worst-pick from the remaining set.

Identifiability: the soft-max is invariant to adding a constant to every \(\beta\), so only \(J - 1 = 9\) of the 10 coefficients are identified. Fix one as the reference — for example, set \(\beta_1 = 0\) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\). Mention this normalization to the reader.
Reshape the data into a form convenient for the likelihood. One workable structure: for each task, store the 4 item numbers, which one was picked best, and which one was picked worst. You’ll iterate over tasks in the likelihood function.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood






Note




Write down the log-likelihood. Summing over \(N \cdot T\) tasks:

\[
\ell(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \;=\; \sum_{\text{tasks}} \Big[\, \beta_{j^*_b} \;-\; \log \!\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown}}\!\!\! \exp(\beta_j) \;-\; \beta_{j^*_w} \;-\; \log \!\!\!\!\!\sum_{j \in \text{shown} \setminus \{j^*_b\}}\!\!\!\! \exp(-\beta_j) \,\Big]
\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the item picked best in that task and \(j^*_w\) is the item picked worst.

Code the log-likelihood as a function ll(beta, ...) where beta is a vector of length 9 (or 10, with \(\beta_1\) pinned to 0 inside the function). Use log-sum-exp form (v - log(rowSums(exp(v)))) for numerical stability — see the week-5 slides on fitting MNL by hand.
Maximize the log-likelihood. In R:

out <- optim(par = rep(0, 9), fn = ll, ...,
             method = "BFGS", hessian = TRUE,
             control = list(fnscale = -1))

Report the estimated \(\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}\) and standard errors. SEs come from the inverse of the negative Hessian:

se <- sqrt(diag(solve(-out$hessian)))

Convert the \(\hat\beta\)’s into shares of preference for interpretability:

\[
\hat s_j \;=\; \frac{\exp(\hat\beta_j)}{\sum_{k=1}^{10} \exp(\hat\beta_k)}
\]
These shares are easier to read than raw \(\beta\)’s — they sum to 1 across items and answer the question, “if all 10 classes were on a single screen, what fraction of choices would each one get?”

Display a table of items with \(\hat\beta_j\), \(SE(\hat\beta_j)\), and \(\hat s_j\). Plot the shares in a horizontal bar chart, sorted from highest to lowest.
Compare the MLE ranking to the counts ranking from the previous section. Are they identical? Where do they differ? Write a few sentences explaining.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Bayesian Estimation






Note




Now fit the same MNL model by Bayesian methods. Reference the week-7 slides on Bayes’ rule and the random-walk Metropolis-Hastings sampler.
The posterior over \(\boldsymbol{\beta}\) is

\[
\pi(\boldsymbol{\beta} \mid \boldsymbol{y}) \;\propto\; L(\boldsymbol{\beta}) \cdot \pi(\boldsymbol{\beta})
\]
Use a weakly informative multivariate normal prior: \(\boldsymbol{\beta} \sim N(\boldsymbol{0},\; 10 \cdot I)\). (SD \(\approx 3.2\) on each \(\beta\) is plenty diffuse without being absurd.)

Code a random-walk Metropolis sampler. The skeleton is:

log_post <- function(beta) ll(beta, ...) + sum(dnorm(beta, 0, sqrt(10), log = TRUE))

mh <- function(beta0, n_iter, step) {
  K <- length(beta0)
  draws <- matrix(NA, n_iter, K)
  beta  <- beta0
  lp    <- log_post(beta)
  accept <- 0
  for (t in 1:n_iter) {
    beta_star <- beta + rnorm(K, 0, step)
    lp_star   <- log_post(beta_star)
    if (log(runif(1)) < lp_star - lp) {
      beta <- beta_star; lp <- lp_star; accept <- accept + 1
    }
    draws[t, ] <- beta
  }
  list(draws = draws, accept_rate = accept / n_iter)
}

Run the sampler for ~20,000 iterations (or more if you have patience). Discard the first 25% as burn-in.
Tune the step size to land at an acceptance rate of roughly 25–40%. If acceptance is too low, halve the step; too high, double it. (See week-7 “Tuning the Step Size” slide.)
Run diagnostics: report the final acceptance rate, plot the trace for at least 2–3 of the \(\beta\) parameters (you should see a “fuzzy caterpillar,” not a slow drift), and confirm that the chain has stabilized after burn-in.
Compute and report:

Posterior mean \(\hat\beta_j\) for each item
95% credible interval \([2.5\%,\, 97.5\%]\) percentiles of the post-burn-in draws
Posterior shares of preference: for each MCMC draw, compute \(\hat s_j^{(s)} = \exp(\hat\beta_j^{(s)}) / \sum_k \exp(\hat\beta_k^{(s)})\), then take the posterior mean of the \(\hat s_j^{(s)}\) across draws.

Display a table with item, posterior mean \(\beta\), 95% CI, posterior mean share. Plot the shares with the credible interval as error bars.
Compare to the MLE results: posterior means should be close to the MLE point estimates; posterior SDs should be close to the MLE standard errors. Where do they differ noticeably? Why?

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Comparing the Three Methods






Note




Create a single side-by-side comparison of all three analyses. Suggested format: a table with columns

Item / class label
Counts score (% best − % worst)
Counts rank
MLE share of preference, MLE rank
Bayes posterior-mean share, Bayes rank

Sorted by one of the rankings (e.g., MLE rank).
Also create a visual comparison: three horizontal bar charts side-by-side, one per method, each showing items sorted by that method’s score/share. Color-code consistently so the reader can trace a single class across all three panels.
Write a paragraph discussing the comparison:

Where do the three methods agree? Almost always at the top and bottom of the ranking.
Where do they disagree — and why? Often in the middle, where items have similar utility and small noise can flip rankings.
What does the MNL add over the counts analysis? (Hint: it models the choice process explicitly — every “shown” item contributes information about not* being picked, not just the picks themselves.)*
What does Bayes add over MLE? (Hint: credible intervals on derived quantities like shares of preference are easy — just push every MCMC draw through the soft-max and take quantiles. Doing this from an MLE requires the delta method.)

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
Discussion






Note




Close with a short discussion section that synthesizes the analysis for a non-technical reader (think: an academic advisor or a student deciding which classes to take). Cover:

The substantive finding — which MSBA classes do students prefer most? Are there any surprises?
The methodological finding — for what kind of question would you reach for each of the three methods?

Counts: quick descriptive summary, useful for an executive dashboard
MLE MNL: rigorous point estimates with classical standard errors; good when you need a single number to report
Bayesian MNL: when you want uncertainty on derived quantities (shares, market simulations, “what if we added a new class?” scenarios), or when you want to extend the model hierarchically to estimate individual-level preferences

Caveats: the survey was on a self-selected sample of MSBA students; preferences may not generalize beyond this group. Item presentation order, prior coursework, and current standing in the program may all affect responses.

End with one or two sentences on what you would do differently or explore next if you had more time.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MaxDiff (also called Best-Worst Scaling) is a survey methodology designed to measure how strongly people prefer one item over another. In each screen of the survey, a respondent sees a small subset of items — here, 4 out of 10 — and selects both the most-preferred and least-preferred item from that subset. This process repeats across many screens, so every item appears alongside many different competitors.
The key advantage of MaxDiff over a standard 1–10 rating scale is that it forces genuine trade-offs. Humans are notoriously poor at calibrating numeric scales consistently — one person’s “8” is another’s “6” — but we are quite good at picking a best and a worst when placed side-by-side. MaxDiff eliminates scale-use bias by replacing ratings with comparisons.
In this assignment, the 10 items are the 10 core MSBA courses at UCSD Rady. We estimate students’ relative preference for each class using three methods:

Counts analysis — the percentage of times each class was picked best minus the percentage picked worst, yielding a simple score between −1 and +1.
MNL via maximum likelihood — a multinomial logit model fit by numerical optimization, producing point estimates with classical standard errors.
MNL via Bayesian estimation — the same MNL model fit by a random-walk Metropolis–Hastings sampler, producing full posterior distributions.

All three methods should agree on the broad ranking of classes. They differ in how they quantify preference strength and how they communicate uncertainty. The main learning goal for me is to build the MNL likelihood and the Metropolis-Hastings sampler directly, instead of treating the model as a black box.

text:
Counts Analysis
The counts analysis provides a quick, model-free summary of preferences. For each item \(j\), we compute the fraction of times it was picked best and the fraction of times it was picked worst (denominator = number of times that item was shown). The score is:
\[
\text{score}_j = \%\text{best}_j - \%\text{worst}_j
\]
A score near +1 means the class is almost always chosen as best whenever shown; near −1 means it is almost always the worst; near 0 means it sits in the undifferentiated middle.


Code
counts = (df_full.groupby("Item")["Response"]
                 .agg(shown="count",
                      best=lambda x: (x == 1).sum(),
                      worst=lambda x: (x == -1).sum())
                 .reset_index())

counts["pct_best"]  = counts["best"]  / counts["shown"]
counts["pct_worst"] = counts["worst"] / counts["shown"]
counts["score"]     = counts["pct_best"] - counts["pct_worst"]
counts = counts.merge(labels, left_on="Item", right_on="item").sort_values("score", ascending=False)

# Vectorised bootstrap using full tasks only (items 1-10)
resp_list = sorted(df["respondent"].unique())
resp_idx  = {r: i for i, r in enumerate(resp_list)}
N_resp    = len(resp_list)

r_arr    = np.array([resp_idx[r] for r in df_full["respondent"]])
item_arr = (df_full["Item"].values - 1).astype(int)   # 0–9 only (no Item 11)
resp_arr = df_full["Response"].values

shown_mat = np.zeros((N_resp, 10), dtype=int)
best_mat  = np.zeros((N_resp, 10), dtype=int)
worst_mat = np.zeros((N_resp, 10), dtype=int)

np.add.at(shown_mat, (r_arr, item_arr), 1)
np.add.at(best_mat,  (r_arr, item_arr), (resp_arr == 1).astype(int))
np.add.at(worst_mat, (r_arr, item_arr), (resp_arr == -1).astype(int))

B = 1000
boot_scores = np.zeros((B, 10))
for b in range(B):
    idx = np.random.randint(0, N_resp, N_resp)
    s  = shown_mat[idx].sum(0)
    be = best_mat[idx].sum(0)
    wo = worst_mat[idx].sum(0)
    boot_scores[b] = (be - wo) / s

ci_lo = np.percentile(boot_scores, 2.5,  axis=0)
ci_hi = np.percentile(boot_scores, 97.5, axis=0)

# Attach CIs (items are 1-indexed)
counts["ci_lo"] = counts["Item"].map(lambda i: ci_lo[i - 1])
counts["ci_hi"] = counts["Item"].map(lambda i: ci_hi[i - 1])

                  counts_sorted["ci_hi"] - counts_sorted["score"]],
            fmt="none", color="gray", linewidth=1.2, capsize=3)
ax.axvline(0, color="gray", linestyle="--", linewidth=0.8)

Business Analytics (MGTA 453) and Customer Analytics (MGTA 455) form the next tier, so students seem to value applied analytics courses that connect directly to business or marketing decisions. Analytics Consulting (MGTA 444), Marketing/Finance/Operations (MGTA 451), and AI Math & Programming (MGTA 403) sit at the bottom. SQL (MGTA 464) and Capstone (MGTA 454) are close to the middle, not clearly loved or disliked by this simple measure. Bootstrap CIs reveal considerable overlap for the middle-ranked items, so I would not over-interpret small rank differences there.

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
section: From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
text:
From MaxDiff Data to MNL Choices
Before fitting a model, we translate the raw survey responses into a form the MNL likelihood can consume. Each MaxDiff task yields two sequential choice observations (per the week-5 MNL slides):

Best pick — from all 4 items shown, which was chosen as best? This is a standard 4-alternative MNL observation.
Worst pick — from the remaining 3 items (best excluded), which was chosen as worst? The key insight is that picking the worst item is equivalent to picking the best item when all utilities are negated. This yields a 3-alternative MNL with flipped signs.

Formally, with utility \(\beta_j\) for item \(j\), the probability that item \(b\) is best from set \(S\) is:
\[
P(\text{best} = b \mid S) = \frac{\exp(\beta_b)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j \in S} \exp(\beta_j)}
\]
Conditional on \(b\) being best, the probability that \(c\) is worst among \(S \setminus \{b\}\) is:
\[
P(\text{worst} = c \mid b, S) = \frac{\exp(-\beta_c)}{\displaystyle\sum_{j \in S \setminus \{b\}} \exp(-\beta_j)}
\]
Identifiability: The softmax is invariant to adding a constant to all \(\beta\)’s, so only \(J - 1 = 9\) parameters are identified. We fix \(\beta_1 = 0\) (MGTA 403 as reference) and estimate \(\beta_2, \ldots, \beta_{10}\).


Code
# Pre-build integer arrays for vectorised log-likelihood
best_items_list  = []
best_choice_list = []
worst_items_list = []
worst_choice_list = []

for (_, _task), g in df_full.groupby(["respondent", "Task"]):
    items     = (g["Item"].values - 1)                # 0-indexed, length 4
    best_idx  = int(g.loc[g["Response"] ==  1, "Item"].iat[0]) - 1
    worst_idx = int(g.loc[g["Response"] == -1, "Item"].iat[0]) - 1
    remain    = items[items != best_idx]               # length 3

    best_items_list.append(items)
    best_choice_list.append(best_idx)
    worst_items_list.append(remain)
    worst_choice_list.append(worst_idx)

best_items_arr   = np.array(best_items_list,  dtype=int)  # (n_tasks, 4)
best_choice_arr  = np.array(best_choice_list, dtype=int)  # (n_tasks,)
worst_items_arr  = np.array(worst_items_list, dtype=int)  # (n_tasks, 3)
worst_choice_arr = np.array(worst_choice_list, dtype=int) # (n_tasks,)

print(f"Total tasks: {len(best_choice_arr)}  "
      f"(→ {len(best_choice_arr) * 2} MNL observations)")

title: MaxDiff Analysis of MSBA Class Preferences
text:
MNL via Maximum Likelihood
The log-likelihood summed over all tasks is:
\[
\ell(\boldsymbol\beta) = \sum_{\text{tasks}} \Bigl[
  \beta_{j^*_b} - \log\!\!\sum_{j \in S}\!\exp(\beta_j)
  \;-\;
  \beta_{j^*_w} - \log\!\!\!\sum_{j \in S \setminus \{j^*_b\}}\!\exp(-\beta_j)
\Bigr]
\]
where \(j^*_b\) is the best-picked item and \(j^*_w\) is the worst-picked item in each task.


Code
def ll(beta_free: np.ndarray) -> float:
    """Log-likelihood for MaxDiff MNL.

    beta_free is the length-9 vector for items 2-10.
    Item 1 is the fixed reference, so beta_1 = 0.
    """
    beta = np.concatenate([[0.0], beta_free])

    # Best pick: log P(best | shown set of 4)
    b_utils  = beta[best_items_arr]                       # (n_tasks, 4)
    ll_best  = beta[best_choice_arr] - logsumexp(b_utils, axis=1)

    # Worst pick: log P(worst | remaining set of 3, negated utilities)
    w_utils  = -beta[worst_items_arr]                     # (n_tasks, 3)
    ll_worst = -beta[worst_choice_arr] - logsumexp(w_utils, axis=1)

    return float(np.sum(ll_best) + np.sum(ll_worst))




Code
result = minimize(
    fun=lambda b: -ll(b),
    x0=np.zeros(9),
    method="BFGS",
    options={"maxiter": 2000},
)

Code
# Numerical Hessian of the negative log-likelihood at the MLE.
# I wrote this directly so the standard errors come from the same objective
# I optimized above, without relying on a separate modeling package.
def approx_hessian(x, f, eps=1e-4):
    x = np.asarray(x, dtype=float)
    K = len(x)
    H = np.zeros((K, K))
    f0 = f(x)

    for i in range(K):
        ei = np.zeros(K)
        ei[i] = eps

        H[i, i] = (f(x + ei) - 2 * f0 + f(x - ei)) / (eps ** 2)

        for j in range(i + 1, K):
            ej = np.zeros(K)
            ej[j] = eps
            H[i, j] = (
                f(x + ei + ej)
                - f(x + ei - ej)
                - f(x - ei + ej)
                + f(x - ei - ej)
            ) / (4 * eps ** 2)
            H[j, i] = H[i, j]

    return H

H   = approx_hessian(result.x, lambda b: -ll(b))
cov = np.linalg.inv(H)
se  = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov))

beta_hat = np.concatenate([[0.0], result.x])
se_hat   = np.concatenate([[np.nan], se])

shares_mle = np.exp(beta_hat) / np.exp(beta_hat).sum()

mle_df = labels.copy()
mle_df["beta"]  = beta_hat
mle_df["se"]    = se_hat
mle_df["share"] = shares_mle
mle_df = mle_df.sort_values("share", ascending=False)
